seo.title: Healthcare Agentic RAG AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for agentic rag AI agents in healthcare. Covers HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, HITECH Act.

content.intro: Deploying Agentic RAG in healthcare environments introduces compounded risk: autonomous query planning across multiple data sources can inadvertently expose PHI, generate hallucinated clinical information, or produce unauditable reasoning chains that violate HIPAA, HITECH, and FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requirements. Unlike static RAG pipelines, agentic systems make multi-step decisions that must each be individually traceable, verifiable, and compliant. This checklist provides concrete controls for every layer of the stack—from PHI handling at ingestion to citation verification at output—to protect patients and your organization from catastrophic regulatory and clinical failures.

sections.heading: PHI Handling & Data Governance
sections.description: Ensure patient health information is protected at every stage of the agentic retrieval pipeline, from document ingestion through LLM synthesis, in compliance with HIPAA Privacy Rule, HITECH Act, and the requirement that PHI never leaves the processing environment.

items.label: Deploy a HIPAA-compliant de-identification pipeline before LLM context injection
items.description: Implement an automated de-identification step (using NLP-based PHI detection tools such as AWS Comprehend Medical or equivalent) that strips or pseudonymizes all 18 HIPAA Safe Harbor identifiers from retrieved document chunks before they are passed to the LLM. Log every de-identification transformation with a before/after hash for audit purposes.

items.label: Enforce data residency: all vector stores and LLM inference must run within your HIPAA-compliant environment
items.description: Confirm that your vector database (e.g., Weaviate, Pinecone Private Cloud, pgvector on-prem), embedding model, and LLM inference endpoint are all hosted within your BAA-covered, network-isolated environment. No PHI-adjacent data should transit to third-party public APIs without a signed Business Associate Agreement and explicit de-identification confirmation.

items.label: Implement field-level encryption for all PHI stored in retrieval indexes
items.description: Encrypt PHI fields at rest using AES-256 within your vector store metadata and document store. Maintain separate encryption keys per patient or per data classification tier, managed through a FIPS 140-2 validated KMS. Ensure encryption keys are rotated on a schedule compliant with your SOC 2 Type II controls.

items.label: Define and enforce role-based access controls (RBAC) on all retrieval data sources
items.description: Map each retrieval source (EHR, clinical notes, lab results, imaging reports) to specific clinical roles. The agentic system must query only sources authorized for the requesting user's role. Implement attribute-based access control (ABAC) policies that the query planner must validate before issuing any sub-query.

items.label: Log all data access events to an immutable audit trail compliant with HIPAA §164.312(b)
items.description: Every document retrieval, chunk access, and metadata lookup performed by the agent must generate a structured audit log entry including: timestamp, user/session ID, data source identifier, query parameters, and records accessed. Store logs in a write-once, tamper-evident system (e.g., AWS CloudTrail with log file validation, or equivalent).

items.label: Establish a data retention and purge policy for agent session context and retrieved chunks
items.description: Define maximum retention periods for in-session retrieved content, intermediate reasoning states, and cached embeddings. Implement automated purge jobs that delete session data after the defined window. Ensure purge events are logged and verifiable for HIPAA minimum necessary standard compliance.

items.label: Validate de-identification effectiveness with a quarterly re-identification risk assessment
items.description: Engage a qualified expert (per HIPAA Expert Determination method, 45 CFR §164.514(b)(1)) to assess whether de-identified outputs from the pipeline could be re-identified using available external datasets. Document findings and remediation actions as part of your HIPAA risk management program.

sections.heading: Agentic Query Planning & Retrieval Integrity
sections.description: Validate that the agent's autonomous query planning, multi-source retrieval, and synthesis steps are accurate, bounded, and produce clinically faithful outputs. Failures in query planning can result in missing critical clinical context or retrieving irrelevant information that misleads clinical decisions.

items.label: Implement a query plan validator that checks each planned sub-query against a clinical ontology whitelist
items.description: Before execution, parse the agent's generated query plan and validate each sub-query against approved clinical terminologies (SNOMED CT, ICD-10, LOINC, RxNorm). Reject or flag queries that reference undefined terms, out-of-scope data sources, or exceed the authorized retrieval scope for the current clinical context.

items.label: Enforce a maximum retrieval depth and source count per agent run to prevent runaway queries
items.description: Configure hard limits on the number of retrieval steps (e.g., max 5 sub-queries per agent invocation), the number of sources queried (e.g., max 4 distinct data sources), and the total token budget for retrieved context. Implement circuit breakers that halt the agent and return a safe fallback response if limits are exceeded.

items.label: Require cross-source consistency checks before synthesizing a final clinical response
items.description: After retrieval from multiple sources, run an automated consistency verification step that detects contradictions between retrieved facts (e.g., conflicting drug dosages, contradictory lab interpretations). Flag inconsistencies in the output with explicit uncertainty markers and require human review before the response is surfaced to a clinician.

items.label: Implement retrieval relevance scoring with a minimum threshold gate
items.description: Score each retrieved chunk for semantic relevance to the clinical query using a dedicated reranker model (e.g., cross-encoder). Discard chunks below a calibrated relevance threshold (validated on your clinical domain). Log discarded chunks and their scores for audit. Never synthesize from low-relevance retrievals in critical clinical contexts.

items.label: Validate query planning accuracy against a labeled clinical QA benchmark before production deployment
items.description: Construct a domain-specific evaluation set of at least 200 clinical queries with ground-truth retrieval plans and expected source sets. Measure query planning accuracy, recall of relevant sources, and precision of retrieved chunks. Establish minimum passing thresholds (e.g., >90% source recall for critical drug/allergy queries) and gate deployment on these metrics.

items.label: Implement a query sanitization layer to prevent prompt injection via retrieved documents
items.description: Scan all retrieved document content for adversarial instruction patterns before injecting into the LLM context. Use a dedicated classifier or rule-based filter to detect and neutralize prompt injection attempts embedded in clinical notes, external references, or user-uploaded documents. Log all detected injection attempts.

items.label: Define and enforce a 'minimum necessary' retrieval policy aligned with HIPAA §164.502(b)
items.description: Configure the query planner to retrieve only the specific fields and document types required to answer the clinical question. Prohibit bulk retrieval of full patient records when only a specific data element is needed. Implement retrieval scope declarations that are logged and reviewable by compliance officers.

sections.heading: Hallucination Detection & Source Faithfulness
sections.description: Clinical AI outputs that contain hallucinated facts—fabricated drug interactions, incorrect dosages, or invented clinical evidence—can directly cause patient harm. Every synthesized response must be verifiably grounded in retrieved sources, with automated detection of unsupported claims.

items.label: Deploy an automated faithfulness scorer on every agent response before delivery
items.description: Integrate a faithfulness evaluation model (e.g., a fine-tuned NLI classifier or a dedicated hallucination detection model such as HHEM or equivalent) that scores each sentence in the agent's response against the retrieved source chunks. Flag any claim with a faithfulness score below your defined threshold (e.g., <0.85) and either block the response or append an explicit uncertainty warning.

items.label: Require inline citation anchoring: every factual claim must reference a specific source chunk with document ID and page/section
items.description: Enforce a structured output format where each factual statement in the synthesized response is annotated with a citation linking to the exact retrieved chunk (document ID, chunk index, and source system). Implement a post-processing validator that rejects responses containing uncited factual claims. Surface citations to end users in a reviewable format.

items.label: Implement a citation verification step that confirms cited sources actually contain the attributed claim
items.description: After response generation, programmatically retrieve each cited chunk and run an entailment check to verify the cited source actually supports the attributed claim. Log verification results. Responses where any citation fails entailment verification must be flagged for human review before clinical use.

items.label: Establish a red-teaming protocol specifically targeting clinical hallucination scenarios
items.description: Before deployment and quarterly thereafter, conduct structured adversarial testing with clinical domain experts targeting known hallucination failure modes: rare drug interactions, off-label dosing, conflicting guideline versions, and ambiguous lab value interpretations. Document findings, failure rates, and mitigations in your risk register.

items.label: Implement confidence calibration and uncertainty quantification for all clinical outputs
items.description: Calibrate the model's confidence scores against empirical accuracy on your clinical validation set. Surface calibrated uncertainty estimates to end users for all responses. Require explicit 'low confidence' warnings when the agent's certainty falls below domain-specific thresholds, and mandate human expert review for low-confidence clinical recommendations.

items.label: Maintain a hallucination incident log and feed confirmed hallucinations back into evaluation benchmarks
items.description: Create a structured process for clinicians to report suspected hallucinations. Log each report with the query, response, retrieved context, and clinical assessment. Confirmed hallucinations must be added to your evaluation benchmark and trigger a root cause analysis. Track hallucination rate as a key operational metric with defined acceptable thresholds.

items.label: Prohibit the agent from synthesizing responses when retrieved context is insufficient to answer the query
items.description: Implement a retrieval sufficiency gate: if the total relevance-weighted retrieved context falls below a minimum coverage threshold for the query, the agent must return a 'insufficient evidence' response rather than generating a potentially hallucinated answer. Define coverage thresholds per query category (e.g., drug dosing, diagnosis, treatment protocol).

sections.heading: Auditability, Human Oversight & Regulatory Compliance
sections.description: HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, and HITECH require that all AI-assisted clinical decisions be fully auditable, attributable, and subject to human oversight. Agentic systems must produce complete, tamper-evident records of their reasoning chains, and must never autonomously execute clinical actions without appropriate human-in-the-loop controls.

items.label: Implement end-to-end reasoning chain logging compliant with FDA 21 CFR Part 11 electronic records requirements
items.description: Capture and store the complete agent execution trace for every clinical query: initial query, generated query plan, each sub-query and its results, intermediate reasoning steps, retrieved chunks with metadata, faithfulness scores, and final synthesized response. Store records with electronic signatures, timestamps, and audit trails in a 21 CFR Part 11 compliant system. Retain for minimum 7 years.

items.label: Enforce a human-in-the-loop review gate for all high-stakes clinical recommendations
items.description: Define a taxonomy of high-stakes query categories (e.g., medication dosing, surgical planning, differential diagnosis, treatment protocol selection). For all queries in these categories, require explicit clinician review and sign-off before the agent's response is acted upon or documented in the EHR. Implement a workflow integration that surfaces the agent's reasoning chain to the reviewing clinician.

items.label: Implement a model card and system card documenting all training data sources, known limitations, and validated use cases
items.description: Produce and maintain a structured model card for the deployed RAG system documenting: training and fine-tuning data provenance, validation dataset composition, performance metrics by clinical domain, known failure modes, intended use cases, and explicitly prohibited use cases. Make this documentation available to clinical staff and compliance reviewers. Update with every model version change.

items.label: Establish a change management process for model updates that includes clinical validation before re-deployment
items.description: Define a formal change control procedure (aligned with FDA 21 CFR Part 11 §11.10(k)) for any update to the LLM, embedding model, retrieval index, or query planning logic. Require re-validation against your clinical benchmark suite, sign-off from clinical informatics and compliance teams, and a documented rollback plan before deploying any change to production.

items.label: Conduct a HIPAA Security Risk Analysis covering the full agentic RAG architecture annually
items.description: Perform a comprehensive risk analysis per HIPAA Security Rule §164.308(a)(1) that specifically addresses agentic system risks: autonomous data access patterns, multi-source retrieval attack surfaces, LLM context window exposure, and vector store security. Document identified risks, likelihood/impact ratings, and implemented safeguards. Update the analysis whenever the architecture changes materially.

items.label: Implement SOC 2 Type II controls for availability, confidentiality, and processing integrity of the agent pipeline
items.description: Map your agentic RAG pipeline components to SOC 2 Trust Service Criteria. Implement specific controls for: availability SLAs with clinical failover procedures, confidentiality controls for PHI in transit and at rest, and processing integrity controls that detect and alert on anomalous agent behavior (e.g., unexpected query volumes, retrieval pattern deviations). Include in your annual SOC 2 audit scope.

items.label: Define and test a clinical AI incident response plan covering agent failures, data breaches, and harmful outputs
items.description: Document a specific incident response playbook for agentic AI failures including: detection criteria for harmful outputs, immediate containment steps (agent shutdown procedure), HIPAA breach notification assessment workflow (60-day notification requirement under HITECH), clinical harm assessment process, and post-incident root cause analysis template. Test the playbook with a tabletop exercise at least annually.

items.label: Implement patient notification and consent mechanisms for AI-assisted clinical decision support
items.description: Establish a process for informing patients when AI-assisted tools have contributed to their clinical care, consistent with emerging state AI transparency laws and HIPAA Notice of Privacy Practices requirements. Document the consent or notification approach, and ensure patients can request human-only review of their care decisions. Log consent status in the patient record.

regulatoryNotes.heading: Regulatory Considerations
notes: HIPAA §164.514(b) requires that PHI be de-identified using either the Safe Harbor method (removing all 18 specified identifiers) or the Expert Determination method (statistical verification of re-identification risk below 0.05) before processing by any LLM. For Agentic RAG, this de-identification must occur at the chunk level before context injection, not just at the document level at ingestion—because retrieved chunks may recombine identifiers that were individually non-identifying.

FDA 21 CFR Part 11 applies to any electronic records and electronic signatures used in clinical decision-making, including AI-generated clinical recommendations that are incorporated into regulated workflows. For Agentic RAG systems used in FDA-regulated contexts (e.g., supporting clinical trial management, drug safety reporting, or regulated diagnostic workflows), the complete agent reasoning chain constitutes an electronic record that must meet Part 11 requirements: audit trails, access controls, record integrity, and retention for the period specified by the applicable predicate rule.

The HITECH Act's Breach Notification Rule (45 CFR §§164.400-414) requires covered entities to notify affected individuals within 60 days of discovering a breach of unsecured PHI. For Agentic RAG systems, a 'breach' can occur through inadvertent PHI exposure in LLM context windows, retrieval of unauthorized records due to access control failures, or exfiltration via prompt injection. Your incident response plan must include a specific assessment workflow to determine whether an agent malfunction constitutes a reportable breach, including documentation of the 'low probability of compromise' safe harbor analysis.

SOC 2 Type II certification requires demonstrating that controls are operating effectively over a minimum 6-month observation period. For Agentic RAG systems, auditors will specifically examine: (1) processing integrity controls that ensure the agent produces complete, accurate, and authorized outputs; (2) confidentiality controls protecting PHI throughout the retrieval pipeline; and (3) availability controls ensuring clinical staff can rely on the system. Ensure your continuous monitoring infrastructure captures agent-specific metrics (query plan accuracy rates, hallucination detection rates, retrieval latency) as evidence for SOC 2 auditors.

cta.heading: Automate Your Healthcare Agent Compliance
cta.description: Deploy Vex's pre-built Healthcare Agentic RAG compliance monitors to automatically enforce PHI de-identification, hallucination detection, citation verification, and 21 CFR Part 11 audit logging across every agent invocation—so your clinical AI ships faster without compromising patient safety or regulatory standing.
